retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Other Issues -- Human Clinical Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Valid informed consent for research must include 3 major elements as follows: Disclosure of information Competency of the patient (or surrogate) to make a decision The voluntary nature of the decision

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Issues of Concern -- Exceptions to Informed Consent
retrieved_docs.content: Several exceptions to the requirement for informed consent include: When the patient is incapacitated In life-threatening emergencies where there is not enough time to obtain consent When consent is voluntarily waived

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Other Issues -- Human Clinical Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Informed consent is mandatory for all clinical trials involving human beings. The consent process must respect the patient's decision-making ability and adhere to the individual hospital rules for clinical studies. Adherence to ethical standards in study design and execution is typically monitored by an Institutional Review Board (IRB). The IRB was established in the United States in 1974 under the National Research Act, which introduced regulations for human research in response to unethical practices, such as those observed in the Tuskegee syphilis experiments. Ethical and safe research standards have been an area of federal and presidential interest since then, with the development of many organizations and task forces since 1974 dedicated to this topic alone. Receiving special attention is the ability or inability of the inmate population to give truly voluntary informed consent to participate in research studies. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Parental Consent -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess the reasons parental consent should be obtained. Identify the historical basis of obtaining informed consent. Determine the common challenging reasons informed parental consent is obtained. Communicate how the healthcare team can work together to obtain parental consent and achieve better outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The Joint Commission requires documentation of all the elements of informed consent in a form, progress notes, or elsewhere in the record. The elements needed for the documentation of the informed consent discussion include: The nature of the procedure or intervention The risks and benefits of the procedure or intervention Reasonable alternatives The risks and benefits of alternatives An assessment of the patient's understanding of these elements [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Rights and Ethics -- Issues of Concern -- Informed Consent Prior to Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: "(T)he patient or his or her representative (as allowed under State law) has the right to make informed decisions regarding his or her care. The patient's rights include being informed of his or her health status (and) being involved in care planning and treatment…"

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the mid-20th century, unethical medical experiments, including the Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis in the Negro Male and the Nazi human experiments during World War II, further underscored the need for stringent consent standards. [1] These events, along with the establishment of the Nuremberg Code and the Declaration of Helsinki, cemented informed consent as a fundamental ethical standard in research and clinical practice. [2] [3] Informed consent has evolved from simply obtaining a patient's signature to a process centered on clear communication. This approach ensures that patients understand the risks, benefits, and alternatives of medical interventions, establishing it as a cornerstone of patient-centered care and medical ethics. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Issues of Concern -- Incomplete Disclosure
retrieved_docs.content: Many states use the reasonable patient standard because it focuses on what a typical patient needs to know to understand the decision at hand. However, it is the sole obligation of the clinician to determine which approach is appropriate for a given situation. [32] [33] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Other Issues -- Human Clinical Studies
retrieved_docs.content: United States federal regulations require a thorough and detailed explanation of the study and its potential risks. An IRB may waive the requirement for informed consent under specific conditions. Paramount to this is the minimal risk to the research participants. An example of minimal risk research is the assessment of interventions that generally occur in emergency situations, such as studying medications used for intubation in the emergency department or conducting a retrospective chart review.

retrieved_docs.title: Parental Consent -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians and medical researchers have a moral and legal obligation to obtain informed consent. Informed consent must include the patient being competent and understanding the options, risks, and benefits. For pediatric patients, parental consent, or consent from a surrogate, must be obtained for medical procedures, treatment, or research. When reasonable, pediatric assent is also a vital part of a positive physician-patient relationship that engages the child in their care. To avoid legal and ethical issues, this activity highlights the role of the interprofessional team in obtaining informed consent from parents.

retrieved_docs.title: Ethical Issues in Academic Medicine -- Issues of Concern -- Informed Consent
retrieved_docs.content: If the IRB decides that research is being performed on human subjects, researchers must ensure that subjects have received properly informed consent. Some exceptions exist, eg, incapacitated patients in emergency research (Langlois), but in general, informed consent is crucial for ethical research. Truly informed consent requires disclosure, understanding, capacity, and voluntariness .

retrieved_docs.title: Research Ethics -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Researchers must also ensure they do not coerce potential participants into agreeing to participate in studies. Coercion refers to threats of penalty, whether implied or explicit, if participants decline to participate or opt out of a study. Additionally, giving potential participants extreme rewards for agreeing to participate can be a form of coercion. The rewards may provide an enticing enough incentive that the participant feels they need to participate. In contrast, they would otherwise have declined if such a reward were not offered. While researchers often use various rewards and incentives in studies, they must carefully review this possibility of coercion. Some incentives may pressure potential participants into joining a study, thereby stripping participants of complete self-determination. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Refusal of Care -- Clinical Significance -- Expressing a Choice
retrieved_docs.content: After discussing the options, which option sounds best for you? Are there things you wouldn't want to be done medically? Is there anyone you with whom would like to discuss your options?

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Rights and Ethics -- Issues of Concern -- Treatment Refusal (by Both Patient and Physician)
retrieved_docs.content: The 1970s to1980s were a time of many US HCO ethics debates and lawsuits regarding patients/families who demanded not to be forced to accept treatments against their wills. In 1986, as part of 42 CFR § 482.13, the president agreed with prior court decisions that any patient who has capacity also has the right to refuse any treatments, regardless of whether other persons deem them to be acts of beneficence. In 1990, the US Supreme Court expanded this right, even for life vs death determining measures, to all Americans via its decision in Cruzan v Director, Missouri Department of Health, 497 U.S. 261. This patient right derives from the principle of autonomy and its associated features, including the right to informed consent.

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Obtaining informed consent in medicine is a process that should include describing the proposed intervention, emphasizing the patient's role in decision-making, discussing alternatives to the proposed intervention, discussing the risks and benefits of the proposed intervention, and eliciting the patient's preference, often confirmed by their signature. Effective informed consent requires a thorough discussion of all relevant risks, which typically encompasses general risks, risks specific to the procedure, risks of no treatment, and treatment alternatives. In addition, many consent forms express no guarantees that the proposed method can resolve the problem being addressed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with الولادة القيصرية (cesarean delivery). The optional procedure إنتهاء الحمل - اختياري (termination of pregnancy – optional) is also clinically associated with الولادة القيصرية, indicating that decisions around pregnancy termination may influence the likelihood of a cesarean section. Additionally, الولادة القيصرية frequently co‑occurs with ربط البوق, suggesting that this specific practice or condition is commonly observed in the context of cesarean deliveries. Recognizing that preterm low‑birth‑weight infants, elective pregnancy termination, and ربط البوق are all linked to cesarean delivery helps clinicians anticipate related risks, plan appropriate obstetric interventions, and provide comprehensive counseling to patients.

These child terms illustrate the breadth of topics covered by the thesaurus, linking psychological considerations such as a patient’s decision to decline therapy with anatomical or procedural terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1799
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) and forceps delivery (الولادة بالملقط) are clinically associated obstetric interventions. Both procedures are employed within the spectrum of pregnancy management: elective termination represents a planned ending of gestation, while forceps delivery is a method of assisted vaginal birth used when specific maternal or fetal conditions warrant intervention. Their clinical association highlights the range of obstetric care options available to address diverse maternal‑fetal indications, and recognizing this relationship assists clinicians in selecting the most appropriate intervention to optimize outcomes for both mother and newborn.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
